skill.name: cavecrew
skill.description: Decision guide for delegating to caveman-style subagents. Tells the main thread WHEN to spawn `cavecrew-investigator` (locate code), `cavecrew-builder` (1-2 file edit), or `cavecrew-reviewer` (diff review) instead of doing the work inline or using vanilla `Explore`. Subagent output is caveman-compressed so the tool-result injected back into main context is ~60% smaller — main context lasts longer across long sessions. Trigger: "delegate to subagent", "use cavecrew", "spawn investigator/builder/reviewer", "save context", "compressed agent output".
skill.summary: Decision guide for delegating to caveman-style subagents for compressed output
skill.author: juliusbrussee

security_audit.summary: The cavecrew skill is a text-based decision guide that instructs AI agents when to delegate work to specialized subagents. After evaluating all 62 static findings (external_commands, filesystem, weak_crypto), all were determined to be false positives. The backtick patterns flagged are markdown inline code formatting, not shell commands. Path traversal flags are relative documentation links. No executable code, network operations, or security-sensitive operations exist in this skill.

high_findings.title: Static Scanner Misidentified Markdown Backticks
high_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' in both README.md and SKILL.md. These are markdown inline code delimiters (e.g., `cavecrew-investigator`, `path:line`), not shell command execution. No user input is passed to any execution context.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backticks are standard markdown code formatting used throughout for subagent names, file paths, and output format examples. No shell or Ruby execution occurs in this skill.

high_findings.title: Static Scanner Misidentified Documentation Links
high_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 'Path traversal sequence' for relative paths like ../../agents/. These are standard markdown links to related documentation files within the repository, not user-controllable path operations.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The ../../agents/ paths are hardcoded relative links to documentation files in the same repository. No path traversal vulnerability exists because these are static documentation links, not runtime path operations.

content.value_statement: Long coding sessions exhaust context window when verbose subagent outputs pile up. Cavecrew provides a decision framework for delegating to specialized caveman-style subagents that return compressed output, extending context lifespan by 60% across many delegations.
actual_capabilities: Decides when to use cavecrew-investigator (code location) vs vanilla Explore
Decides when to use cavecrew-builder (1-2 file edit) vs main thread
Decides when to use cavecrew-reviewer (diff review) vs Code Reviewer
Defines output contracts for each subagent (path:line format, summary formats)
Describes chaining patterns: locate-fix-verify, parallel scout, single-shot edit
Explains context token savings from compressed subagent output
limitations: This is a decision guide only - it does not execute code or spawn agents directly
Output is terse and structured, not prose - may require paraphrasing for human readers
cavecrew-builder refuses 3+ file scope and returns 'too-big.' instead of executing

use_cases.title: Long coding sessions with many delegations
use_cases.description: When running Claude Code or Codex on large codebases across many turns, use cavecrew subagents to keep context window from exhausting. Each delegation saves ~60% tokens vs vanilla equivalents.
use_cases.target_user: AI coding assistant users working on large projects

use_cases.title: Code investigation with known scope
use_cases.description: Need to find where a function is defined or where something is called? Spawn cavecrew-investigator to get compressed path:line results that integrate cleanly into the main conversation.

use_cases.title: Quick diff review after edits
use_cases.description: Made changes and want a quick sanity check? Call cavecrew-reviewer on the diff to get severity-coded findings without the verbosity of a full code review.
use_cases.target_user: Developers wanting fast feedback on changes

prompt_templates.title: Basic code location
prompt_templates.prompt: Where is the authenticateUser function defined in this codebase?

prompt_templates.title: Find and fix
prompt_templates.prompt: Find all uses of the deprecated helper function and update them to use the new one at src/utils/newHelper.ts

prompt_templates.title: Review changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the changes in this branch for common bugs and style issues

prompt_templates.title: Parallel investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to refactor the payment module. Find the main entry point, all its callers, and its test files in parallel.

output_examples.input: Where is the handleClick function defined?
output_examples.output: src/components/Button.tsx:42 — handleClick — onClick handler. src/hooks/useButton.ts:12 — useButton — wrapper hook. totals: 2 files.

output_examples.input: Update the error logging in auth.ts to include user ID
output_examples.output: src/auth.ts:28-32 — add userId to error log. verified: re-read OK.
src/auth.ts:45: 🟡 Low: Missing null check. Add guard before accessing token. src/api.js:12: 🔵 Style: Prefer const over let. totals: 1🟡 1🔵.

best_practices: Use cavecrew when you need the output compressed. Use vanilla Explore/Code Reviewer when you want prose and architecture commentary.
Chain investigators to builders to reviewers: locate specific sites, make targeted edits, verify with a diff review.
Use parallel scout with 2-3 investigators when investigating from multiple angles (definitions, callers, tests) before deciding on a refactor.
anti_patterns: Do not use cavecrew-builder without knowing the file - spawn investigator first or waste tokens passing context.
Do not chain investigators and builders for 5-file refactors - builder will return 'too-big.' and waste a turn.
Do not ask cavecrew-reviewer for 'general feedback' - it returns structured findings only, no opinions or rationale.

faq.question: What is the context token savings from using cavecrew?
faq.answer: Cavecrew subagents return output compressed to approximately 1/3 the size of vanilla equivalents. Across 20 delegations, this can mean the difference between context exhaustion and completing the task.

faq.question: When should I use vanilla Explore instead of cavecrew-investigator?
faq.answer: Use vanilla Explore when you want architecture commentary, suggestions, or prose explanations. Use cavecrew-investigator when you need fast, structured code location data that fits into a token budget.

faq.question: Can cavecrew-builder handle large refactors?
faq.answer: No. cavecrew-builder is designed for surgical 1-2 file edits with obvious scope. For 3+ files or cross-cutting changes, use the main thread or a feature architect agent.

faq.question: How does cavecrew-reviewer differ from the Code Reviewer agent?
faq.answer: cavecrew-reviewer returns one-line findings with severity emoji in a structured format. Code Reviewer provides deep review with rationale, alternatives, and prose explanations.

faq.question: What triggers cavecrew to activate?
faq.answer: Phrases like 'delegate to subagent', 'use cavecrew', 'spawn investigator', 'save context', or 'compressed agent output' activate the skill.

faq.question: Is cavecrew a slash command or automatic behavior?
faq.answer: Cavecrew is a decision guide that activates on certain phrases. It instructs the main agent on when to spawn its subagents, not a command you invoke directly.
